file_name: Cerus Corporation - FIRST AMEND TO SUPPLY AND MANUFACTURING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise expressly provided in the applicable Statement of Work payment to Porex of undisputed fees shall be due [*] days following Cerus' receipt of the invoice submitted by Porex.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless otherwise expressly provided in the applicable Statement of Work, payment to Porex of undisputed fees shall be due approximately [*] days following Cerus' receipt of the invoice submitted by Porex.
perturbation.explanation: The word 'approximately' introduces ambiguity regarding the payment deadline. This makes it unclear when payment is definitively due, potentially leading to late payment disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law mandates specific payment timelines for public works contracts. Introducing 'approximately' directly contradicts the requirement for clear and fixed payment deadlines. This could lead to legal challenges concerning late payment penalties and interest.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, PCC 10262.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Public Contract Code - PCC DIVISION 2. GENERAL PROVISIONS [1100 - 22355] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1981, Ch. 306. ) PART 2. CONTRACTING BY STATE AGENCIES [10100 - 19150] ( Heading of Part 2 added by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1120, Sec. 6. ) CHAPTER 1. State Contract Act [10100 - 10285.5] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1981, Ch. 306. ) ARTICLE 8. Modifications; Performance; Payment [10250 - 10265] ( Article 8 enacted by Stats. 1981, Ch. 306. ) 10262.5. (a) Notwithstanding any other law, a prime contractor or subcontractor shall pay to any subcontractor, not later than seven days after receipt of each progress payment, the respective amounts allowed the contractor on account of the work performed by the subcontractors, to the extent of each subcontractor’s interest therein. In the event that there is a good faith dispute over all or any portion of the amount due on a progress payment from the prime contractor or subcontractor to a subcontractor, then the prime contractor or subcontractor may withhold no more than 150 percent of the disputed amount. Any contractor who violates this section shall pay to the subcontractor a penalty of 2 percent of the amount due per month for every month that payment is not made. In any action for the collection of funds wrongfully withheld, the prevaili
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces the word 'approximately,' creating ambiguity regarding the payment deadline, which contradicts the requirement for clear and fixed payment deadlines. California Code, PCC 10262.5(a) indicates that a prime contractor or subcontractor shall pay any subcontractor, not later than seven days after receipt of each progress payment. By using 'approximately' in the changed text it potentially weakens the obligation to make timely payments.

perturbation.original_text: Cerus shall have the right to accept or reject the Services, or any portion thereof, in writing within [*] days from Porex's completion of the Services. Such acceptance or rejection shall be consistent with the criteria set forth in the Statement of Work, if any.
perturbation.changed_text: Cerus shall have the right to accept or reject the Services, or any portion thereof, in writing within [*] days from Porex's substantial completion of the Services. Such acceptance or rejection should generally align with the criteria set forth in the Statement of Work, if any.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'completion' to 'substantial completion' introduces ambiguity about the precise point at which the acceptance/rejection window begins. Replacing 'shall be consistent' with 'should generally align' weakens the requirement for consistency, allowing for deviation from the agreed-upon criteria in the Statement of Work, opening the door to subjective interpretations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - Acceptance of Goods
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC requires clear standards for acceptance or rejection of goods or services. By using 'substantial completion' and 'should generally align', the agreement creates uncertainty about the conditions under which Cerus is obligated to accept the services, contradicting the UCC's goal of providing a predictable framework for commercial transactions.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'completion' to 'substantial completion' introduces ambiguity regarding when the acceptance/rejection window begins. Replacing 'shall be consistent' with 'should generally align' weakens the consistency requirement, potentially allowing deviations from the Statement of Work's criteria. According to the LLM law explanation, this change goes against the UCC's aim of offering a clear and predictable structure for commercial transactions. While the scraped snippets are not usable, the provided Perturbation and Law explanations clearly indicate the original contract's standard of completion, and strict consistency criteria, was undermined by the changes creating ambiguity and weakening obligations for service acceptance. This introduction of ambiguity directly contradicts the need for a predictable framework.

perturbation.original_text: For disputed invoices or the disputed portion of an invoice, Cerus shall use reasonable efforts to provide to Porex, in writing, within [*] days, a description of the disputed amounts. Cerus and Porex shall negotiate in a timely, good faith manner to resolve billing queries.
perturbation.changed_text: For disputed invoices or the disputed portion of an invoice, Cerus may attempt to provide to Porex, in writing, within approximately [*] days, a description of the disputed amounts. Cerus and Porex should endeavor to negotiate to address billing queries.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall use reasonable efforts' to 'may attempt' weakens the obligation to provide a description of the disputed amounts. Also, 'shall negotiate in a timely, good faith manner' is modified to 'should endeavor to negotiate to address billing queries', this weakens the negotiation obligation. The words 'approximately' and 'endeavor' also add ambiguity and make the obligation less strict.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Virtually all contracts are understood to include an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, meaning parties must act honestly and reasonably in performing their contractual obligations. Weakening the obligation to provide a description of disputed amounts and the obligation to negotiate disputes could be interpreted as a breach of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, particularly if Cerus consistently fails to provide timely or adequate explanations for disputed invoices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces "shall use reasonable efforts" with "may attempt" and "shall negotiate in a timely, good faith manner" with "should endeavor to negotiate." This significantly weakens the obligations of Cerus. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." The original text aligns with this obligation of good faith by mandating reasonable efforts and timely negotiation. The changed text, by making these actions optional and less stringent, contradicts the obligation of good faith as it allows for the possibility of not acting honestly and reasonably in performing contractual obligations, potentially leading to non-compliance with the UCC.
